title: How to Spot Phishing Scams and Protect Your Money: A Complete Guide
excerpt: Learn to identify phishing emails, texts, and calls before they steal your money. Practical tips to protect your bank accounts and personal information.
content:
## Introduction

Your phone buzzes. It is a text from your bank warning about suspicious activity on your account. There is a link to verify your identity. You click it without thinking, enter your login details, and within minutes, your account is drained.

This is phishing in action. It is one of the most common and devastating financial scams, and it happens to thousands of people every single day.

The good news? Phishing scams follow predictable patterns. Once you know what to look for, you can spot them instantly. This guide will teach you exactly how to identify phishing attempts across email, text, phone calls, and social media, so you can keep your money safe. For a broader overview of financial fraud, see our guide on [how to protect your money from scams](/blog/protect-your-money-from-scams-2026).

## What Is Phishing?

Phishing is a type of fraud where criminals pretend to be legitimate organizations to steal your personal information. They want your passwords, credit card numbers, Social Security number, or bank login credentials.

The name comes from "fishing" because scammers cast wide nets, sending millions of fake messages hoping someone will take the bait.

### Common Types of Phishing

**Email Phishing**: The classic approach. You receive an email that looks like it is from your bank, Amazon, Netflix, or the IRS. It claims there is a problem with your account and urges you to click a link to fix it.

**Smishing (SMS Phishing)**: Phishing via text message. These often claim to be delivery notifications, bank alerts, or prize winnings.

**Vishing (Voice Phishing)**: Phone calls from scammers pretending to be tech support, government agencies, or your bank. They create urgency to get you to share information or send money.

**Spear Phishing**: Targeted attacks using personal information about you. These are harder to spot because they reference real details from your life.

**Social Media Phishing**: Fake profiles, hacked accounts, or malicious links shared through platforms like Facebook, Instagram, or LinkedIn.

## Red Flags That Scream "Phishing"

Most phishing attempts share common warning signs. Learn these, and you will catch 90% of scams before they catch you.

### 1. Urgency and Fear Tactics

Phishing messages almost always create panic. They want you to act fast, before you have time to think.

Watch for phrases like:
- "Your account will be suspended in 24 hours"
- "Immediate action required"
- "Your payment failed, update now"
- "Unauthorized access detected"
- "Verify your identity or lose access"

Legitimate companies rarely demand immediate action via email or text. If a message makes your heart race, pause. That is exactly what scammers want.

### 2. Suspicious Sender Addresses

Always check who actually sent the message. Scammers use addresses that look similar to real ones but contain slight differences.

Examples of fake sender addresses:
- support@amaz0n.com (zero instead of "o")
- alerts@bankofamerica.secure-login.com (real domain would end in bankofamerica.com)
- service@paypa1.com (number one instead of "l")
- noreply@apple.com.suspicious-domain.net (real domain buried in the middle)

On mobile devices, tap the sender name to reveal the actual email address. On computers, hover over it.

### 3. Generic Greetings

Your bank knows your name. So does Netflix, Amazon, and any company you have an account with.

Phishing emails often use:
- "Dear Customer"
- "Dear Account Holder"
- "Dear User"
- "Hello Friend"

If a company you do business with sends a generic greeting, that is a warning sign.

### 4. Spelling and Grammar Mistakes

Professional companies have editors. Phishing emails often have obvious errors:
- Misspelled words
- Awkward phrasing
- Random capitalization
- Missing punctuation

One typo might be a mistake. Multiple errors in an "official" email are a red flag.

### 5. Suspicious Links

Never click links in suspicious messages. Instead, hover over them (do not click) to see where they actually lead.

Red flags in URLs:
- Misspelled company names
- Extra words or numbers
- HTTP instead of HTTPS
- Unfamiliar domains
- URL shorteners (bit.ly, tinyurl) in official communications

When in doubt, go directly to the company's website by typing the address in your browser. Do not use the link in the email.

### 6. Requests for Sensitive Information

Legitimate companies will never ask for:
- Your full password
- Your PIN
- Your Social Security number via email
- Credit card details in a reply
- Verification codes sent to your phone

If a message asks for this information, it is almost certainly a scam.

### 7. Unexpected Attachments

Be extremely cautious with email attachments, especially if you were not expecting them. Malicious attachments can install malware that steals your information or locks your files for ransom.

Dangerous file types include:
- .exe (executable programs)
- .zip (compressed files that hide other files)
- .doc or .xls with macros
- .pdf (can contain malicious code)

Even if an attachment appears to come from someone you know, verify with them directly before opening it.

## How to Protect Yourself

Knowing the warning signs is half the battle. Here is how to build strong defenses against phishing attacks.

### Enable Two-Factor Authentication

[Two-factor authentication](/blog/what-is-two-factor-authentication-protect-financial-accounts) (2FA) adds a second layer of security to your accounts. Even if a scammer gets your password, they cannot access your account without the second factor.

Use 2FA on:
- Your email accounts
- Bank and financial accounts
- Social media
- Shopping sites like Amazon
- Any account with sensitive information

Prefer authenticator apps (like Google Authenticator or Authy) over SMS codes when possible. SIM swapping attacks can intercept text message codes.

### Use Unique Passwords for Every Account

If you use the same password everywhere, one successful phishing attack compromises all your accounts. Use a password manager to create and store unique, strong passwords for each site.

Popular password managers include:
- 1Password
- Bitwarden (free option)
- Dashlane
- LastPass

### Verify Directly with the Company

If you receive a suspicious message about your account, do not use any links or phone numbers in that message. Instead:

1. Open a new browser window
2. Type the company's official website address
3. Log in to your account directly
4. Check for any alerts or issues
5. Call the company using the phone number on their official website

### Keep Software Updated

Security updates patch vulnerabilities that hackers exploit. Keep your operating system, browser, and apps updated. Enable automatic updates when possible.

### Use Email Filtering

Most email providers have spam and phishing filters. Make sure they are enabled. You can also report phishing emails to help improve these filters for everyone.

In Gmail, click the three dots next to a suspicious email and select "Report phishing." In Outlook, select the message and click "Report" then "Report phishing."

## What to Do If You Fall for a Phishing Scam

If you clicked a link or entered information before realizing it was a scam, act fast.

### Immediate Steps

1. **Change your passwords immediately**. Start with the compromised account, then any accounts using the same password.

2. **Contact your bank**. If you entered financial information, call your bank right away. They can freeze your accounts and watch for suspicious activity. If your [credit card is stolen](/blog/what-to-do-if-credit-card-stolen) or compromised, follow the proper steps to report it immediately.

3. **Enable fraud alerts**. Contact one of the three credit bureaus (Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion) to place a fraud alert on your credit report. Learn more about [protecting yourself from identity theft](/blog/how-to-protect-yourself-from-identity-theft).

4. **Monitor your accounts**. Check your bank statements and credit reports regularly for the next few months.

5. **Run a security scan**. Use antivirus software to check for malware on your device.

### Report the Scam

Reporting helps authorities track scammers and warn others:

- **FTC**: Report at ReportFraud.ftc.gov
- **FBI**: Report at ic3.gov
- **Anti-Phishing Working Group**: Forward phishing emails to reportphishing@apwg.org

## Real-World Phishing Examples

Understanding how phishing works in practice makes it easier to spot. Here are common scenarios.

### The Fake Bank Alert

You receive a text: "ALERT: Your Chase account has been locked due to suspicious activity. Click here to verify: chasebank-secure.com/verify"

Red flags:
- The domain is not chase.com
- Creates urgency with "locked" language
- Asks you to click a link

What to do: Ignore the text. Log into your Chase account directly through the app or website to check for issues.

### The Package Delivery Scam

An email arrives: "Your package could not be delivered. Click here to reschedule: usps-delivery-update.com"

Red flags:
- Generic message with no tracking number
- Domain is not usps.com
- You were not expecting a package

What to do: If you are expecting a delivery, go directly to the carrier's website and enter your tracking number.

### The Tech Support Call

Someone calls claiming to be from Microsoft. They say your computer has a virus and offer to fix it if you give them remote access.

Red flags:
- Microsoft does not make unsolicited calls
- They are asking for remote access to your computer
- They may ask for payment via gift cards

What to do: Hang up immediately. Microsoft will never call you about computer problems.

## Teaching Your Family

Phishing does not just target you. Elderly parents and young adults are often vulnerable too.

### For Older Adults

- Explain that legitimate organizations will not ask for personal information by phone or email
- Help them set up 2FA on important accounts
- Create a rule: always call a family member before sending money or sharing information
- Write down the real phone numbers for their bank and doctor

### For Teenagers and Young Adults

- Show them examples of phishing messages
- Explain how social media information can be used against them
- Help them set up password managers
- Discuss the financial consequences of identity theft

## Using Technology to Stay Safe

Beyond your own vigilance, technology can help protect you.

### Browser Security

Modern browsers warn you about suspicious websites. Pay attention to these warnings. If your browser says a site is dangerous, do not proceed.

### Anti-Phishing Software

Many antivirus programs include anti-phishing features that check links before you click them. Consider using:

- Norton 360
- Bitdefender
- Malwarebytes

### Email Security

Some email clients offer enhanced phishing protection. Business email services like Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace have advanced threat detection.

## Track Your Financial Safety with Pennywise

Staying on top of your finances helps you spot unauthorized transactions quickly. When you track every expense with Pennywise, you will notice immediately if something does not look right.

Regular monitoring is one of the best defenses against financial fraud. If a scammer does get access to your accounts, catching it within hours instead of days can save you thousands.

## Key Takeaways

Phishing scams are everywhere, but they are not hard to spot once you know the patterns.

Remember these essentials:

1. **Pause before you panic**. Urgency is a scammer's favorite tool. Take a breath before clicking anything.

2. **Check the sender**. Look at the actual email address, not just the display name.

3. **Never click suspicious links**. Go directly to company websites instead.

4. **Protect yourself proactively**. Use 2FA, unique passwords, and keep software updated.

5. **When in doubt, verify**. Call the company directly using a number you find yourself.

6. **Act fast if you fall for one**. Change passwords, contact your bank, and monitor your accounts.

Your money is worth protecting. A few seconds of verification can save you from months of financial recovery.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### How can I tell if an email is really from my bank?

Check the sender's email address carefully. Banks send from their official domain (like @bankofamerica.com), not random addresses. They will address you by name, never ask for passwords via email, and will not demand immediate action. When in doubt, call your bank using the number on your card.

### What should I do if I accidentally clicked a phishing link?

If you only clicked the link but did not enter information, run an antivirus scan immediately. If you entered login credentials, change that password right away, then change it on any other accounts using the same password. If you entered financial information, contact your bank immediately.

### Can phishing happen on my smartphone?

Yes. Smishing (SMS phishing) is increasingly common. Scammers send text messages with malicious links. Be just as careful with texts as you are with emails. Do not click links from unknown numbers or unexpected messages.

### How do I report a phishing attempt?

Forward phishing emails to reportphishing@apwg.org. Report phishing to the FTC at ReportFraud.ftc.gov. You can also report to the company being impersonated, as most have security teams that track these scams.

### Why do phishing scams still work?

Scammers are sophisticated. They create convincing replicas of real websites and emails. They exploit emotions like fear and urgency. And they only need a small percentage of people to fall for a scam to make money. That is why education and vigilance remain the best defenses.
